text: The robotic surgical procedure involves mobilizing the ligament of Treitz. The surgeon liberates the fourth portion of the duodenum and the first portion of the jejunum from the retroperitoneal space.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to carefully dissect around the superior mesenteric vein and artery, which are visible traversing into the small bowel. The instrument functions as both a bipolar artery sealer and a scissor, allowing precise division of tissue in this delicate area.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the jejunum from the retroperitoneum, preparing for its eventual mobilization and coparization with the duodenum. This segment is part of the specimen and distal margin to be removed and resected.
The surgeon uses a GIA stapler with a tan load to divide the jejunum, further dividing the mesentery as high as possible in this portion.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the fourth portion of the duodenum, aiming to position it beneath the mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon divides the antrum of the stomach, with the lesser sac already opened. A vessel sealer is passed behind the stomach to create a tunnel for the GIA stapler, which is then carefully placed and used to divide the stomach. The vessel sealer is shown angulating and reticulating to facilitate this process.

The surgeon ensures no surrounding tissue is inadvertently grasped by the GIA stapler before firing. The GIA stapler, equipped with three rows of staples on either side, cuts between them to complete the division of the stomach using a second load.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to grasp the gastroduodenal artery, which is seen branching from the common hepatic artery. Further mobilization of the hepatic artery is performed.

The surgeon performs a lymph node dissection along the hepatic artery, which is clipped from the gastrodial artery, extending proximally to the celiac axis. The dissection reveals multiple lymph nodes being harvested.

The surgeon dissects at the level of the hepatic duct and common bile duct, with the cystic duct being grasped for retraction and visualization. This maneuver provides a clear view of the portal vein, which is located to the right of the spatula cautery.

The robotic instruments are used to slide a spatula underneath the junction of the common hepatic duct and cystic duct. The hepatic artery is visible with its branch extending to the right lobe of the liver, identified as the right hepatic artery. Lymph nodes and the portal vein are seen tethering the common hepatic duct and common bile duct.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to divide the common hepatic duct, with a visible stent placed preoperatively to address biliary obstruction. The bipolar grasper protects the portal vein from cautery injury during the division. Upon completion, the portal triad and surrounding lymph nodes are clearly visible.

The robotic instruments meticulously dissect the hepatic ducts and portal vein, which are closely involved with surrounding structures. The surgeon performs careful lymph node dissections, considering the patient's pancreatic cancer diagnosis.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric vein below the caudal border of the pancreas, using a suction device to protect it from cautery. The inferior pancreatic duodenal artery is cauterized with a bipolar device, minimizing blood loss. The robotic approach aids in cautious dissection, revealing the pancreatic duct with clear fluid indicating its presence. Further division of the pancreas enhances visualization of the cut surface.

The surgeon approaches the cranial aspect of the pancreatic division, encountering the superior pancreaticoduodenal artery. Hemostasis is achieved promptly. The superior mesenteric vein transitions into the portal vein in this region.

The surgeon liberates the superior mesenteric vein from its lateral attachments to the pancreas, with the pancreas and uncinate process positioned more posteriorly. The procedure progresses to cocorizing the duodenum, revealing the superior mesenteric artery and vein beneath the vessel sealer.

The surgeon continues to divide attachments to further mobilize the duodenum, progressing from the third to the fourth portion. The robotic approach allows precise manipulation of the delicate duodenal structures, utilizing wrist rotation for careful dissection.

The surgeon retracts tissue to reveal a hint of the jejunum during further dissection. The initial mobilization of the ligament triads aids in this part of the procedure. The view shifts cranially to re-identify the superior mesenteric vein, following it caudally to ensure thorough lymph node harvesting.

The surgeon mobilizes the jejunum underneath the root of the mesentery, working within two portions of the abdominal cavity: below and above the transverse mesocolon. The open approach allows for direct visualization and secure manipulation of the jejunum as it is passed underneath the superior mesenteric vein and artery.

The surgeon retracts the jejunum from underneath the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and superior mesenteric artery (SMA), working exclusively in the upper abdomen. The mesentery is rotated, and vascular attachments are released under direct visualization.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to immobilize small blood vessels that were previously difficult to visualize. The GIA stapler with gray vascular loads is then employed to capture and secure these vessels leading into the uncinate process.
The surgeon meticulously harvests lymph nodes along the lateral aspect of the superior mesenteric artery, ensuring optimal preservation of oncological principles during the procedure.

The surgeon visually identifies the superior mesenteric artery, noting a large branch that has been previously taken. The artery is visibly pulsating, and a small branch is prepared for division using a GIA stapler. This view is unique to the current surgical approach.

The surgical field extends cranially towards the porta hepatis, with careful attention to the vascular supply to achieve the widest radial margin possible. The superior mesenteric vein is visible, and its resection with reconstruction is considered feasible with this approach.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction phase, with the posterior fixation of the pancreas to the jejunum already completed. The focus is now on performing the anastomosis between the pancreatic duct and the mucosa of the jejunum.

The surgeon places the posterior stitch to enhance visualization of the anatomical structures. The pancreatic duct appears decompressed with a stent, measuring approximately three millimeters in diameter. The suture being used is visibly large but theoretically as thin as a hair.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to place sutures with precise millimeter-by-millimeter accuracy, tying them to exact tension and cutting them meticulously. The three-dimensional view from the console enhances the precision of these actions.

The needle can be grasped at different angles and manipulated in different ways. Not only does the optics afford great magnification, but the optics on the console, again, are much greater than the ones that we see on the screen here. between the wristedness of the instruments and the ability to see this really affords the perfect combination that allows us to do this operation.

The surgeon performs suturing at the ten o'clock position, taking a bite on the pancreas. After a brief camera clean, a bite is taken on the jejunum, and the suture is withdrawn to the appropriate length. The suture is not tied immediately to avoid obscuring the placement of the next stitch. The suture at the two o'clock position is then placed with a healthy bite on the jejunum, and the previously placed suture is tied.

The surgeon places and ties the final suture at the twelve o'clock position of the pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis. The suture is completed without the use of stents, which some practitioners prefer but are deemed unnecessary in this procedure.

The surgeon uses a B-lock suture with barbs to create the top row of the pancreatic anastomosis, ensuring tension is relieved and the anastomosis is buttressed. The completed anastomosis is visible, and attention shifts to the hepaticojejunostomy, which is facilitated by the larger size of the bile duct.
The surgeon places a drain near the hepatic duct, which is visibly larger than the pancreatic duct. An aneurotomy is made in the jejunum to perform a single-layer interrupted anastomosis.

The surgeon begins the biliary anastomosis at the five o'clock position, using between six to eight sutures to complete the procedure. Protraction is applied to enhance visibility of the surgical field and ensure precise placement of sutures around the clock face.
